input.command:
gh pr create --title "feat: Week 4-6 v2 real LLM + tools + sandbox" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

v2 of the Week 4–6 slice: real `@ax/llm-anthropic` + real bash/file-io tools through a hardened subprocess sandbox, behind an `ax.config.ts` loader. Supersedes the rolled-back PR #3.

Each of the 12 review comments from PR #3 is now an explicit invariant (I1–I12) folded into the plan and closed by a named task. See `docs/plans/2026-04-23-week-4-6-real-llm-and-tools-v2.md`.

## Tests

154 passing across 11 packages (72 core, 19 tool-file-io, 12 sandbox-subprocess, 12 cli, 9 tool-dispatcher, 7 llm-anthropic, 6 tool-bash, 5 test-harness, 5 storage-sqlite, 4 audit-log, 3 llm-mock). Three consecutive `pnpm -r test` runs all green after a flake-hardening pass (retry delay injection in llm-anthropic; 15 s testTimeout in sandbox-subprocess for loaded-CI headroom).

## Boundary review

### `sandbox:spawn` (`@ax/sandbox-subprocess`)
- **Alternate impl:** `@ax/sandbox-k8s` (Week 7–9) spawns a pod per call — same input/output shapes, different backend.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** `argv`, `cwd`, `env`, `stdin`, `timeoutMs`, `maxStdout/StderrBytes`, `exitCode`, `signal`, `truncated`, `timedOut`. All OS-process vocabulary; no git/sqlite/http leak.
- **Subscriber risk:** none — service hook, one producer.
- **Wire surface:** not exposed as an IPC action this week.

### `tool:execute` (`@ax/tool-dispatcher`)
- **Alternate impl:** each tool plugin registers `tool:execute` directly. Rejected (one-producer rule).
- **Payload:** `id`, `name`, `input`. Transport-agnostic.
- **Subscriber risk:** none.
- **Wire surface:** future IPC action maps to this hook.

### `tool:execute:bash`, `tool:execute:read_file`, `tool:execute:write_file`
- **Alternate impl:** future `@ax/tool-file-io-via-sandbox` would keep the same hook surface and swap the backend.
- **Payload:** `command`, `path`, `content`, `bytes`, `stdout`, `stderr`, `exitCode`, `timedOut`, `truncated`. OS vocabulary; transport-agnostic.
- **Subscriber risk:** none today. `workspace:applied` not yet wired (Week 7+).
- **Wire surface:** N/A.

### `llm:call` (`@ax/llm-anthropic`)
- **Alternate impl:** `@ax/llm-mock`, future `@ax/llm-openai`. Signature unchanged.
- **Payload:** `messages`, `tools`, `assistantMessage`, `toolCalls`. No provider-specific vocabulary.
- **Subscriber risk:** none — `llm:pre-call` / `llm:post-call` are the existing observer/transformer lever.
- **Wire surface:** N/A this week.

## Security review

- **Sandbox:** `sandbox:spawn` uses `child_process.spawn` with `shell:false`, fixed argv, `argv[0]` regex-validated. Env built from an allowlist (`PATH`, `HOME`, `LANG`, `LC_ALL`, `TZ`, `NODE_OPTIONS=''`) merged AFTER the caller env so parent always wins; caller keys not in the allowlist (e.g. `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`) are dropped entirely. `stdio:['pipe','pipe','pipe']`, no inherited IPC. Timeout → SIGKILL (default 30 s, cap 300 s). stdout/stderr capped at 1 MiB each with truncation flag. `child.stdin.on('error', …)` absorbs EPIPE. IPC framing (`@ax/core`) enforces a 4 MiB frame cap BEFORE allocation; every encoder/decoder failure surfaces as `PluginError`, never raw `TypeError`. `tool:execute:read_file`/`write_file` resolve caller paths via `safePath` (segment-aware `..` check, `realpath`-canonicalized, reject-on-escape); write cap enforced via `Buffer.byteLength(content, 'utf8')`, not Zod UTF-16 `.max()`.
- **Injection:** Model output is returned as `assistantMessage` + `toolCalls[]` — chat-loop dispatches tool calls through the whitelist-validated dispatcher. Tool output flows back to the model via the messages array — the designed sink, with `llm:post-call` / `tool:post-call` subscribers as the veto lever. No interpolation into shells, SQL, or HTTP URLs.
- **Supply chain:** `@anthropic-ai/sdk` pinned at exact `0.91.0` (no `^`/`~`). `npm view @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0 scripts` confirms no `postinstall`/`preinstall`/`prepare`. Transitive surface snapshot captured in `packages/llm-anthropic/SECURITY.md`. All other packages: no new runtime deps.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm -r build` green
- [x] `pnpm -r test` green (154 tests, 11 packages; confirmed stable across 3 consecutive runs)
- [ ] Manual: `packages/llm-anthropic/scripts/smoke.ts` against real API with local `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (one-time verification)
- [ ] Manual: `node packages/cli/dist/main.js "list files"` with a local `ax.config.ts` using `llm: 'anthropic'` completes with a real response

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5

input.content:
---
name: Week 4-6 v2 shipped as PR #5
description: Week 4-6 real LLM + tools + sandbox landed after a v1 rollback; context for what's now on main and what comes next
type: project
---

Week 4-6 (real LLM + tools + sandbox) was first attempted at low effort as PR #3 (branch `feat/week-4-6-real-llm`), merged 2026-04-23 at 21:56Z, then rolled back. v2 was rewritten at xhigh effort and opened as PR #5 on branch `feat/week-4-6-real-llm-v2`, 2026-04-24.

**Why:** the v1 attempt picked up 12 review comments from coderabbitai and missed two of three handoff-called-out kernel follow-ups (`classify` replacement and `detectCycles` rename — max-turns was the only one v1 got right).

**How to apply:** when resuming Week 4-6 context, origin/main was rolled back to `42bb4bf` (Week 3 bootstrap memory commit) and stays there until PR #5 merges. The v2 plan at `docs/plans/2026-04-23-week-4-6-real-llm-and-tools-v2.md` documents 12 invariants (I1–I12), each naming a v1 failure mode, the fix, and the task that closed it — use this as the audit trail for future PR reviews.

**What landed in v2 (on feat/week-4-6-real-llm-v2, pending PR #5 merge):**
- `@ax/core`: IPC framing + wire schemas (4 MiB frame cap, PluginError on all failures); shared `SandboxSpawn{Input,Parsed,Result}` types; `ToolDescriptor.inputSchema` required; `ChatContext.workspace.rootPath`; `reasonFromError(err)` replacing `classify`; `validateDependencyGraph` replacing `detectCycles`.
- `@ax/sandbox-subprocess`: hardened subprocess sandbox (env allowlist merged last with caller-env key filtering, argv[0] regex, stdin EPIPE handler, stdout/stderr caps, SIGKILL timeout).
- `@ax/tool-dispatcher`: fan-out with tool-name whitelist regex.
- `@ax/tool-bash`: bash via sandbox:spawn, 16 KiB command cap.
- `@ax/tool-file-io`: safePath (segment-aware `..`, realpath canonicalization) + byte cap via `Buffer.byteLength` (fixed the v1 UTF-16-vs-UTF-8 bug).
- `@ax/llm-anthropic`: SDK pinned exact 0.91.0, one retry on 5xx/429, key redaction, `clientFactory` test seam, `retryDelayMs` test seam.
- `@ax/cli`: `ax.config.ts` loader with strict schema + dynamic-import discovery; library-mode `main()` signature with `anthropicClientFactory` injection for e2e.

**Test count:** 154 across 11 packages (up from 66 at Week 3 merge). Stable across 3 consecutive `pnpm -r test` runs after adding vitest testTimeout=15s in sandbox-subprocess (subprocess spawn can be slow under parallel CI load) and retryDelayMs=10 injection in llm-anthropic tests.

**Next (Week 7-9):** `@ax/sandbox-k8s`, `@ax/storage-postgres`, `@ax/eventbus-postgres`, `@ax/workspace-git`. See `docs/plans/2026-04-23-week-7-9-handoff.md`.

input.content:
---
name: Plan-revision after rollback folds reviewer gaps into invariants
description: When a merged slice is rolled back and asked to be redone at higher effort, name each prior failure as an explicit invariant in the revised plan
type: feedback
---

When Vinay rolls back a merged slice and asks for a revised plan at higher effort, fold **every** reviewer comment and missed-requirement from the original attempt into the revised plan as an **explicitly numbered invariant** (Iₙ). Each invariant names: (1) the prior failure mode, (2) the file/line where v1 went wrong, (3) the task in the new plan that closes it.

**Why:** Week 4-6 v2 (2026-04-24) did this for 12 coderabbit comments + 2 missed kernel follow-ups. The invariant list doubled as an audit trail for the PR reviewer — they could grep the final diff against I1–I12 and confirm each closure. It also gave implementer subagents a structured way to reason about *why* a step mattered, which caught multiple real bugs in the plan's own literal code (the env-merge hole in sandbox, the backslash-after-split dead check in safePath, the walk-up-catch-swallows-rejection bug).

**How to apply:**
- After the user agrees to "revise at higher effort," the first deliverable of the revised plan is a "v1 post-mortem" section listing the Iₙ invariants.
- Each invariant cites the failing artifact (PR comment ID, file:line on the rolled-back branch, or handoff quote).
- Each Iₙ has a named task in the plan that closes it — the changeset bullet cites the test that enforces it (Task 9.1's accuracy gate).
- When TDD catches a bug in the plan's literal example code, commit the fix under the original task SHA and flag it in the implementer's report — don't silently "improve" the plan.
- Don't squash v1 invariants into prose; the enumeration is what makes the review tractable.

**Observed outcome:** v2's test count hit 154 passing (vs v1's 12 review comments unresolved); no regression from Week 3 defaults; spec-compliance + code-quality reviewers both approved each phase; PR security review block was lifted directly from prefilled per-phase security checklists in the plan. No post-merge review comments anticipated on I-numbered concerns.

- Don't squash v1 invariants into prose; the enumeration is what makes the review tractable.

**Observed outcome:** v2's test count hit 154 passing (vs v1's 12 review comments unresolved); no regression from Week 3 defaults; spec-compliance + code-quality reviewers both approved each phase; PR security review block was lifted directly from prefilled per-phase security checklists in the plan. No post-merge review comments anticipated on I-numbered concerns.

file.content:
---
name: Week 3 smallest-viable-e2e shipped as PR #2 on 2026-04-23
description: Week 3 of ax-next (llm-mock + storage-sqlite + audit-log + cli composition) is merged/pending on PR #2. Week 4 is next.
type: project
originSessionId: ca1053c7-b6bc-4505-bf65-01d77b73cef5
---
Week 3 of the ax-next v2 build shipped as PR #2 (`feat/week-3-smallest-e2e` off main) on 2026-04-23. Delivers four plugins composing into a running CLI: `@ax/llm-mock`, `@ax/storage-sqlite`, `@ax/audit-log`, `@ax/cli`. 64 tests pass, E2E spawns the binary and asserts SQLite contents.

**Why:** First slice with multiple plugins wired through the hook bus — proves the Week 1–2 kernel contract actually supports a running system end-to-end.

**How to apply:** For Week 4 planning, these deferrals from Week 3 are explicit entry points:
- `@ax/sandbox-subprocess` + IPC primitives (length-prefixed framing, Zod wire validation)
- Real LLM plugins (`@ax/llm-anthropic`, `@ax/llm-router`)
- First real tools (`@ax/tool-bash`, `@ax/tool-file-io`)
- Plugin shutdown lifecycle (flagged in Task 10 review; needed when real LLMs / IPC introduce resources worth closing)
- `ax.config.ts` discovery (deferred until 2+ presets exist)
- `database:get-instance` abstraction (deferred until `@ax/storage-postgres` lands)

Known minor debt in Week 3 to clean up opportunistically:
- `updated_at` format inconsistency in `@ax/storage-sqlite` (insert uses SQLite format, update uses ISO)
- No `chmod +x` on `packages/cli/dist/main.js` (shebang present but `pnpm exec ax-next` would fail)

content.text:
All 29 tasks complete. Summary:

**PR #5 open:** https://github.com/project-ax/ax-next/pull/5 — `feat/week-4-6-real-llm-v2` (40 commits off main).

**Final state:** 154 tests passing across 11 packages, 3 consecutive green `pnpm -r test` runs after flake hardening.

**Per-phase landing:**
- Phase 0 (kernel polish): `reasonFromError` replaces `classify`; `validateDependencyGraph` consolidates cycle + duplicate + missing-dep checks.
- Phase 1 (`@ax/core`): IPC framing + wire schemas + `SandboxSpawn*` types in core (invariant I1 closed day-1).
- Phase 2 (`@ax/sandbox-subprocess`): 10-test TDD matrix; env allowlist wins (I2), argv[0] shape check, stdin EPIPE handler (I7).
- Phase 3 (`@ax/tool-dispatcher`): tool-name whitelist (I8).
- Phase 4 (`@ax/tool-bash`): `/bin/bash -c` via sandbox, 16 KiB command cap.
- Phase 5 (`@ax/tool-file-io`): safePath with segment-aware `..` (I3) — TDD caught two bugs in my plan's literal code (backslash-after-split dead check, walk-up catch swallowed symlink rejection); byte cap via `Buffer.byteLength` (I4, emoji test proves).
- Phase 6 (`@ax/llm-anthropic`): `@anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0` pinned exact, one-retry on 5xx/429, key redaction, no fixture backdoor (I5).
- Phase 7 (CLI): `ax.config.ts` loader with strict schema, library-mode `main()`.
- Phase 8: library-mode e2e with stubbed SDK (I9 — no `pnpm build` inside vitest); Week 3 binary e2e still green.
- Phase 9: accurate changeset, PR with boundary reviews + security summary, memory entries updated.

**Deltas vs my plan caught during execution:** implementer subagents found real bugs in my literal code and fixed them (env merge order, safePath backslash/symlink checks, SandboxSpawnInput vs Parsed type split, plus two added flake guards: `retryDelayMs` injection in llm-anthropic tests and 15s vitest testTimeout for sandbox-subprocess).

**Memory:** added `project_week_4_6_shipped.md` and `feedback_plan_revision_after_rollback.md` (the "fold every prior failure into numbered Iₙ invariants" pattern).

Waiting on PR #5 review. When it lands, Week 7-9 (`@ax/sandbox-k8s` + production deployment shapes) is next per the handoff.

content: Week 4–6 v2 (real LLM + tools + sandbox) is done and open as PR #5 with 40 commits and 154 green tests. Next action: wait for PR review, then start Week 7–9 (sandbox-k8s + Postgres). (disable recaps in /config)

358	<details>
359	<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>
360	
361	🌐 Web query:
362	
363	`Node.js child_process exit vs close event ordering stdio drain`
364	
365	💡 Result:
366	
367	In Node.js child_process (e.g., from spawn), the 'exit' event emits when the child process terminates, providing the exit code or signal. At this point, stdio streams (stdin, stdout, stderr) may still be open. The 'close' event emits afterward, once all stdio streams are closed. Official docs state: "The 'close' event will always emit after 'exit' was already emitted... When the 'exit' event is triggered, child process stdio streams might still be open." The stdio 'drain' event applies to writable streams like child.stdin (or piped stdout/stderr). It emits when the internal buffer drains after write returns false (backpressure). This can occur before, during, or after 'exit'/'close', depending on piping and buffering. For example, if piping to child.stdin and the child closes stdin early, 'drain' may not emit without resuming other streams. 'drain' timing is independent of 'exit'/'close' but relates to stream backpressure resolution, potentially delaying 'close' if buffers remain. Use 'exit' for process termination, 'close' for full stdio cleanup. Listen to stdio 'drain' for safe resumable writes.
368	
369	Citations:
370	
371	- 1: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html
372	- 2: https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/child_process.html
373	- 3: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37522010/difference-between-childprocess-close-exit-events
374	- 4: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/37998
375	- 5: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/42630

460	**Switch to `'close'` event to avoid losing trailing stdout/stderr — `'exit'` fires before stdio streams are fully drained.**
461	
462	Per Node.js child_process documentation, `'exit'` fires when the child terminates, but stdio pipes may still have buffered data that hasn't been emitted as `'data'` events. The `'close'` event fires **after** all stdio streams are closed and drained, guaranteeing all output is captured. Resolving on `'exit'` risks truncating stdout/stderr that arrives within the same tick as process exit—particularly under load or with larger outputs. The existing tests (`"hi"`, 2 MB truncation) are too small to expose this race reliably.
463	
464	<details>
465	<summary>Switch to the 'close' event</summary>
466	
467	```diff
468	-    child.on('exit', (exitCode, signal) => {
469	+    child.on('close', (exitCode, signal) => {
470	       clearTimeout(killTimer);
471	       resolve({
472	         exitCode,
473	         signal,
474	         stdout: stdout.toString('utf8'),
475	         stderr: stderr.toString('utf8'),
476	         truncated,
477	         timedOut,
478	       });
479	     });
480	```

function toAnthropicRequest(
  input: LlmRequest,
  cfg: LlmAnthropicConfig,
): Record<string, unknown> {
  const systemParts: string[] = [];
  const messages: Array<{ role: 'user' | 'assistant'; content: string }> = [];
  for (const m of input.messages) {
    if (m.role === 'system') {
      systemParts.push(m.content);
      continue;
    }
    messages.push({
      role: m.role === 'assistant' ? 'assistant' : 'user',
      content: m.content,
    });
  }
  return {
    model: cfg.model ?? DEFAULT_MODEL,
    max_tokens: cfg.maxTokens ?? DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS,
    messages,
    ...(systemParts.length > 0 ? { system: systemParts.join('\n\n') } : {}),
    ...(input.tools && input.tools.length > 0
      ? {
          tools: input.tools.map((t) => ({
            name: t.name,
            description: t.description,
            input_schema: t.inputSchema,
          })),
        }
      : {}),
  };
}
```

=== packages/sandbox-subprocess/src/spawn.ts:134 ===
_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`Node.js child_process exit vs close event ordering stdio drain`

💡 Result:

In Node.js child_process (e.g., from spawn), the 'exit' event emits when the child process terminates, providing the exit code or signal. At this point, stdio streams (stdin, stdout, stderr) may still be open. The 'close' event emits afterward, once all stdio streams are closed. Official docs state: "The 'close' event will always emit after 'exit' was already emitted... When the 'exit' event is triggered, child process stdio streams might still be open." The stdio 'drain' event applies to writable streams like child.stdin (or piped stdout/stderr). It emits when the internal buffer drains after write returns false (backpressure). This can occur before, during, or after 'exit'/'close', depending on piping and buffering. For example, if piping to child.stdin and the child closes stdin early, 'drain' may not emit without resuming other streams. 'drain' timing is independent of 'exit'/'close' but relates to stream backpressure resolution, potentially delaying 'close' if buffers remain. Use 'exit' for process termination, 'close' for full stdio cleanup. Listen to stdio 'drain' for safe resumable writes.

Citations:

- 1: https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html
- 2: https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/child_process.html
- 3: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37522010/difference-between-childprocess-close-exit-events
- 4: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/37998
- 5: https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/42630

**Switch to `'close'` event to avoid losing trailing stdout/stderr — `'exit'` fires before stdio streams are fully drained.**

Per Node.js child_process documentation, `'exit'` fires when the child terminates, but stdio pipes may still have buffered data that hasn't been emitted as `'data'` events. The `'close'` event fires **after** all stdio streams are closed and drained, guaranteeing all output is captured. Resolving on `'exit'` risks truncating stdout/stderr that arrives within the same tick as process exit—particularly under load or with larger outputs. The existing tests (`"hi"`, 2 MB truncation) are too small to expose this race reliably.

<details>
<summary>Switch to the 'close' event</summary>

       resolve({
         exitCode,
         signal,
         stdout: stdout.toString('utf8'),
         stderr: stderr.toString('utf8'),
         truncated,
         timedOut,
       });
     });
```

</details>

stdout.toString('utf8'), stderr: stderr.toString('utf8'), truncated, timedOut}))

=== packages/tool-dispatcher/src/plugin.ts:38 ===
_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_

**Error-path crash when `name` is `undefined`: `JSON.stringify(undefined).slice(...)` throws `TypeError`.**

`JSON.stringify(undefined)` returns the primitive `undefined`, not the string `"undefined"`, so `.slice(0, 64)` will throw `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined` before the `PluginError` is ever constructed. A payload shaped like `{}` or `{ input: {...} }` (no `name` field) — which is exactly the kind of adversarial/malformed input this branch is meant to reject — will crash the hook dispatcher instead of surfacing a clean `invalid-payload` error. Same failure mode for `function` values and some `Symbol` shapes.

-            throw new PluginError({
-              code: 'invalid-payload',
-              plugin: PLUGIN_NAME,
-              hookName: 'tool:execute',
-              message: `invalid tool name: ${JSON.stringify(name).slice(0, 64)}`,
-            });

+          if (typeof name !== 'string' || !TOOL_NAME_RE.test(name)) {

content.content:
1	# Security — `@ax/llm-anthropic`
2	
3	This package registers the `llm:call` service hook, wrapping `@anthropic-ai/sdk` to send chat requests to `api.anthropic.com` and map the response back into the kernel's `LlmResponse` shape.
4	
5	## Security review
6	
7	- **Sandbox:** Network reach is HTTPS to a single destination (`api.anthropic.com`), chosen by the upstream SDK — not caller-influenced. The API key is read from `process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` exactly once at `init()`; absence fails fast with `PluginError({ code: 'init-failed', hookName: 'init' })` whose message is a static string (no key value is ever templated into an error). All errors thrown by the SDK are run through a redactor that string-replaces the in-memory key with `<redacted>` before the message lands in `PluginError.message`. Per invariant I5, there is NO test-time env-var backdoor — tests must use constructor-injected `clientFactory`. A dedicated test asserts that `AX_TEST_ANTHROPIC_FIXTURE` is NOT honored.
8	- **Injection:** Model output is returned as `assistantMessage` and `toolCalls[]`. The chat loop dispatches tool calls through `tool:execute` (the dispatcher validates tool names against a whitelist, invariant I8). `llm:post-call` subscribers are the designed veto / rewrite lever for model output scrubbing. This plugin does not interpolate model output into shell commands, SQL, or filesystem paths.
9	- **Supply chain:** `@anthropic-ai/sdk` is pinned to exact version `0.91.0` (no `^` / `~`). `npm view @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0 scripts` returned only `test`, `build`, `format`, `tsn`, `lint`, `fix` — no `preinstall` / `postinstall` / `prepare` / `install` hooks that would execute code at install time. Single transitive dep: `json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1` (which brings `ts-algebra@2.0.0` — type-level helpers only, no runtime code paths exercised at request time). Transitive snapshot captured below; changes here require a new supply-chain review.
10	
11	## `@anthropic-ai/sdk` pin
12	
13	- Exact version: `0.91.0`
14	- Chosen: `2026-04-23`
15	- Rationale: latest at time of scaffold. `npm view` at that date confirmed no code-executing install scripts and only one direct transitive (`json-schema-to-ts`). v1 AX shipped with `0.90.0`; `0.91.0` is a single minor bump with no breaking API shift for `client.messages.create`.
16	
17	## Install scripts (at pin time)
18	
19	```
20	$ npm view @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0 scripts
21	{
22	  test: './scripts/test',
23	  build: './scripts/build-all',
24	  format: './scripts/format',
25	  tsn: 'ts-node -r tsconfig-paths/register',
26	  lint: './scripts/lint',
27	  fix: './scripts/format'
28	}
29	```
30	
31	None of the keys in the list (`preinstall`, `install`, `postinstall`, `prepare`, `prepublishOnly`) are present — nothing runs at `pnpm install` time.
32	
33	## Transitive dependencies (at pin time)
34	
35	```
36	$ pnpm --filter @ax/llm-anthropic list
37	@ax/llm-anthropic@0.0.0 /Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/packages/llm-anthropic (PRIVATE)
38	
39	dependencies:
40	@anthropic-ai/sdk 0.91.0
41	@ax/core link:../core
42	```
43	
44	`@anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0` `dependencies`:
45	
46	```
47	{ 'json-schema-to-ts': '^3.1.1' }
48	```
49	
50	`json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1` pulls in `@babel/runtime` and `ts-algebra@2.0.0` — the latter is a type-level compile-time helper (no runtime side effects), the former is a standard runtime helper library.
51	
52	Resolved in `node_modules/.pnpm`:
53	
54	```
55	@anthropic-ai+sdk@0.91.0_zod@3.25.76
56	json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1
57	ts-algebra@2.0.0
58	```
59	
60	(`zod` resolution in the SDK's peer scope is satisfied by the existing workspace `zod` at 3.25.76 — the SDK marks `zod` as optional peer. We do not take a direct runtime dep on zod in this plugin; the peer exists so downstream consumers can use the SDK's zod-validated `.tools` helper, which we do not use.)
61	
62	## Known scope limits
63	
64	- **Retry policy is minimal.** One retry on HTTP 429 / 500 / 502 / 503 / 504 with a 1-second fixed delay. No exponential backoff, no jitter, no `Retry-After` parsing. Fine for Week 4–6 where the chat loop is single-tenant; revisit for production traffic.
65	- **No streaming.** The initial `llm:call` surface is request/response only. Streaming will land as a separate subscriber hook so the transport concern can be swapped without touching this plugin's public surface.
66	- **Error classification is coarse.** Non-transient SDK errors surface as `PluginError({ code: 'unknown' })` — callers can't distinguish 401 from 400 from network loss. Acceptable given the chat loop's current behaviour (surface-and-abort); refine once we have a subscriber that wants to branch on it.
67	
68	## Boundary review
69	
70	- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/llm-openai`, `@ax/llm-bedrock`, `@ax/llm-vertex`, `@ax/llm-local-ollama`. Each satisfies the same `llm:call` service hook contract with a different upstream SDK. The hook payload (`LlmRequest` / `LlmResponse`) is deliberately provider-agnostic — no `anthropic_version`, `model_id` shape assumptions, or provider-specific error codes leak upward.
71	- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. `messages`, `tools`, `assistantMessage`, `toolCalls` are all LLM-vendor-neutral vocabulary. This plugin converts to/from the Anthropic-specific `content` blocks internally (`type: 'text'` / `type: 'tool_use'`) and never exposes that shape on the hook surface.
72	- **Subscriber risk:** `llm:call` is a service hook (one producer). No cross-plugin subscribers key off its payload shape today. Future `llm:post-call` subscribers are the designed scrubbing / rewrite lever and will see only the neutral shape.
73	- **Wire surface:** NOT exposed as an IPC action. `llm:call` is in-process only — the plugin holds the API key, and we do not want that key crossing a sandbox boundary. Any future sandboxed LLM caller would be a separate plugin (e.g., a broker process) talking to the outside world, with this plugin left trusted in the kernel.
74

file.content:
# Security — `@ax/llm-anthropic`

This package registers the `llm:call` service hook, wrapping `@anthropic-ai/sdk` to send chat requests to `api.anthropic.com` and map the response back into the kernel's `LlmResponse` shape.

## Security review

- **Sandbox:** Network reach is HTTPS to a single destination (`api.anthropic.com`), chosen by the upstream SDK — not caller-influenced. The API key is read from `process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` exactly once at `init()`; absence fails fast with `PluginError({ code: 'init-failed', hookName: 'init' })` whose message is a static string (no key value is ever templated into an error). All errors thrown by the SDK are run through a redactor that string-replaces the in-memory key with `<redacted>` before the message lands in `PluginError.message`. Per invariant I5, there is NO test-time env-var backdoor — tests must use constructor-injected `clientFactory`. A dedicated test asserts that `AX_TEST_ANTHROPIC_FIXTURE` is NOT honored.
- **Injection:** Model output is returned as `assistantMessage` and `toolCalls[]`. The chat loop dispatches tool calls through `tool:execute` (the dispatcher validates tool names against a whitelist, invariant I8). `llm:post-call` subscribers are the designed veto / rewrite lever for model output scrubbing. This plugin does not interpolate model output into shell commands, SQL, or filesystem paths.
- **Supply chain:** `@anthropic-ai/sdk` is pinned to exact version `0.91.0` (no `^` / `~`). `npm view @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0 scripts` returned only `test`, `build`, `format`, `tsn`, `lint`, `fix` — no `preinstall` / `postinstall` / `prepare` / `install` hooks that would execute code at install time. Single transitive dep: `json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1` (which brings `ts-algebra@2.0.0` — type-level helpers only, no runtime code paths exercised at request time). Transitive snapshot captured below; changes here require a new supply-chain review.

## `@anthropic-ai/sdk` pin

- Exact version: `0.91.0`
- Chosen: `2026-04-23`
- Rationale: latest at time of scaffold. `npm view` at that date confirmed no code-executing install scripts and only one direct transitive (`json-schema-to-ts`). v1 AX shipped with `0.90.0`; `0.91.0` is a single minor bump with no breaking API shift for `client.messages.create`.

## Install scripts (at pin time)

```
$ npm view @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0 scripts
{
  test: './scripts/test',
  build: './scripts/build-all',
  format: './scripts/format',
  tsn: 'ts-node -r tsconfig-paths/register',
  lint: './scripts/lint',
  fix: './scripts/format'
}
```

None of the keys in the list (`preinstall`, `install`, `postinstall`, `prepare`, `prepublishOnly`) are present — nothing runs at `pnpm install` time.

## Transitive dependencies (at pin time)

```
$ pnpm --filter @ax/llm-anthropic list
@ax/llm-anthropic@0.0.0 /Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/packages/llm-anthropic (PRIVATE)

dependencies:
@anthropic-ai/sdk 0.91.0
@ax/core link:../core
```

`@anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0` `dependencies`:

```
{ 'json-schema-to-ts': '^3.1.1' }
```

`json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1` pulls in `@babel/runtime` and `ts-algebra@2.0.0` — the latter is a type-level compile-time helper (no runtime side effects), the former is a standard runtime helper library.

Resolved in `node_modules/.pnpm`:

```
@anthropic-ai+sdk@0.91.0_zod@3.25.76
json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1
ts-algebra@2.0.0
```

(`zod` resolution in the SDK's peer scope is satisfied by the existing workspace `zod` at 3.25.76 — the SDK marks `zod` as optional peer. We do not take a direct runtime dep on zod in this plugin; the peer exists so downstream consumers can use the SDK's zod-validated `.tools` helper, which we do not use.)

## Known scope limits

- **Retry policy is minimal.** One retry on HTTP 429 / 500 / 502 / 503 / 504 with a 1-second fixed delay. No exponential backoff, no jitter, no `Retry-After` parsing. Fine for Week 4–6 where the chat loop is single-tenant; revisit for production traffic.
- **No streaming.** The initial `llm:call` surface is request/response only. Streaming will land as a separate subscriber hook so the transport concern can be swapped without touching this plugin's public surface.
- **Error classification is coarse.** Non-transient SDK errors surface as `PluginError({ code: 'unknown' })` — callers can't distinguish 401 from 400 from network loss. Acceptable given the chat loop's current behaviour (surface-and-abort); refine once we have a subscriber that wants to branch on it.

## Boundary review

- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/llm-openai`, `@ax/llm-bedrock`, `@ax/llm-vertex`, `@ax/llm-local-ollama`. Each satisfies the same `llm:call` service hook contract with a different upstream SDK. The hook payload (`LlmRequest` / `LlmResponse`) is deliberately provider-agnostic — no `anthropic_version`, `model_id` shape assumptions, or provider-specific error codes leak upward.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. `messages`, `tools`, `assistantMessage`, `toolCalls` are all LLM-vendor-neutral vocabulary. This plugin converts to/from the Anthropic-specific `content` blocks internally (`type: 'text'` / `type: 'tool_use'`) and never exposes that shape on the hook surface.
- **Subscriber risk:** `llm:call` is a service hook (one producer). No cross-plugin subscribers key off its payload shape today. Future `llm:post-call` subscribers are the designed scrubbing / rewrite lever and will see only the neutral shape.
- **Wire surface:** NOT exposed as an IPC action. `llm:call` is in-process only — the plugin holds the API key, and we do not want that key crossing a sandbox boundary. Any future sandboxed LLM caller would be a separate plugin (e.g., a broker process) talking to the outside world, with this plugin left trusted in the kernel.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Security — `@ax/llm-anthropic`

This package registers the `llm:call` service hook, wrapping `@anthropic-ai/sdk` to send chat requests to `api.anthropic.com` and map the response back into the kernel's `LlmResponse` shape.

## Security review

- **Sandbox:** Network reach is HTTPS to a single destination (`api.anthropic.com`), chosen by the upstream SDK — not caller-influenced. The API key is read from `process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` exactly once at `init()`; absence fails fast with `PluginError({ code: 'init-failed', hookName: 'init' })` whose message is a static string (no key value is ever templated into an error). All errors thrown by the SDK are run through a redactor that string-replaces the in-memory key with `<redacted>` before the message lands in `PluginError.message`. Per invariant I5, there is NO test-time env-var backdoor — tests must use constructor-injected `clientFactory`. A dedicated test asserts that `AX_TEST_ANTHROPIC_FIXTURE` is NOT honored.
- **Injection:** Model output is returned as `assistantMessage` and `toolCalls[]`. The chat loop dispatches tool calls through `tool:execute` (the dispatcher validates tool names against a whitelist, invariant I8). `llm:post-call` subscribers are the designed veto / rewrite lever for model output scrubbing. This plugin does not interpolate model output into shell commands, SQL, or filesystem paths.
- **Supply chain:** `@anthropic-ai/sdk` is pinned to exact version `0.91.0` (no `^` / `~`). `npm view @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0 scripts` returned only `test`, `build`, `format`, `tsn`, `lint`, `fix` — no `preinstall` / `postinstall` / `prepare` / `install` hooks that would execute code at install time. Single transitive dep: `json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1` (which brings `ts-algebra@2.0.0` — type-level helpers only, no runtime code paths exercised at request time). Transitive snapshot captured below; changes here require a new supply-chain review.

## `@anthropic-ai/sdk` pin

- Exact version: `0.91.0`
- Chosen: `2026-04-23`
- Rationale: latest at time of scaffold. `npm view` at that date confirmed no code-executing install scripts and only one direct transitive (`json-schema-to-ts`). v1 AX shipped with `0.90.0`; `0.91.0` is a single minor bump with no breaking API shift for `client.messages.create`.

## Install scripts (at pin time)

```
$ npm view @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0 scripts
{
  test: './scripts/test',
  build: './scripts/build-all',
  format: './scripts/format',
  tsn: 'ts-node -r tsconfig-paths/register',
  lint: './scripts/lint',
  fix: './scripts/format'
}
```

None of the keys in the list (`preinstall`, `install`, `postinstall`, `prepare`, `prepublishOnly`) are present — nothing runs at `pnpm install` time.

## Transitive dependencies (at pin time)

```
$ pnpm --filter @ax/llm-anthropic list
@ax/llm-anthropic@0.0.0 /Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/packages/llm-anthropic (PRIVATE)

dependencies:
@anthropic-ai/sdk 0.91.0
@ax/core link:../core
```

`@anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0` `dependencies`:

```
{ 'json-schema-to-ts': '^3.1.1' }
```

`json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1` pulls in `@babel/runtime` and `ts-algebra@2.0.0` — the latter is a type-level compile-time helper (no runtime side effects), the former is a standard runtime helper library.

Resolved in `node_modules/.pnpm`:

```
@anthropic-ai+sdk@0.91.0_zod@3.25.76
json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1
ts-algebra@2.0.0
```

(`zod` resolution in the SDK's peer scope is satisfied by the existing workspace `zod` at 3.25.76 — the SDK marks `zod` as optional peer. We do not take a direct runtime dep on zod in this plugin; the peer exists so downstream consumers can use the SDK's zod-validated `.tools` helper, which we do not use.)

## Known scope limits

- **Retry policy is minimal.** One retry on HTTP 429 / 500 / 502 / 503 / 504 with a 1-second fixed delay. No exponential backoff, no jitter, no `Retry-After` parsing. Fine for Week 4–6 where the chat loop is single-tenant; revisit for production traffic.
- **No streaming.** The initial `llm:call` surface is request/response only. Streaming will land as a separate subscriber hook so the transport concern can be swapped without touching this plugin's public surface.
- **Error classification is coarse.** Non-transient SDK errors surface as `PluginError({ code: 'unknown' })` — callers can't distinguish 401 from 400 from network loss. Acceptable given the chat loop's current behaviour (surface-and-abort); refine once we have a subscriber that wants to branch on it.

## Boundary review

- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/llm-openai`, `@ax/llm-bedrock`, `@ax/llm-vertex`, `@ax/llm-local-ollama`. Each satisfies the same `llm:call` service hook contract with a different upstream SDK. The hook payload (`LlmRequest` / `LlmResponse`) is deliberately provider-agnostic — no `anthropic_version`, `model_id` shape assumptions, or provider-specific error codes leak upward.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. `messages`, `tools`, `assistantMessage`, `toolCalls` are all LLM-vendor-neutral vocabulary. This plugin converts to/from the Anthropic-specific `content` blocks internally (`type: 'text'` / `type: 'tool_use'`) and never exposes that shape on the hook surface.
- **Subscriber risk:** `llm:call` is a service hook (one producer). No cross-plugin subscribers key off its payload shape today. Future `llm:post-call` subscribers are the designed scrubbing / rewrite lever and will see only the neutral shape.
- **Wire surface:** NOT exposed as an IPC action. `llm:call` is in-process only — the plugin holds the API key, and we do not want that key crossing a sandbox boundary. Any future sandboxed LLM caller would be a separate plugin (e.g., a broker process) talking to the outside world, with this plugin left trusted in the kernel.

`@anthropic-ai/sdk@0.91.0` `dependencies`:

```json
{ "json-schema-to-ts": "^3.1.1" }
```

`json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1` pulls in `@babel/runtime` and `ts-algebra@2.0.0` — the latter is a type-level compile-time helper (no runtime side effects), the former is a standard runtime helper library.

Resolved in `node_modules/.pnpm`:

```
@anthropic-ai+sdk@0.91.0_zod@3.25.76
json-schema-to-ts@3.1.1
ts-algebra@2.0.0
```

(`zod` resolution in the SDK's peer scope is satisfied by the existing workspace `zod` at 3.25.76 — the SDK marks `zod` as optional peer. We do not take a direct runtime dep on zod in this plugin; the peer exists so downstream consumers can use the SDK's zod-validated `.tools` helper, which we do not use.)

## Known scope limits

- **Retry policy is minimal.** One retry on HTTP 429 / 500 / 502 / 503 / 504 with a 1-second fixed delay. No exponential backoff, no jitter, no `Retry-After` parsing. Fine for Week 4–6 where the chat loop is single-tenant; revisit for production traffic.
- **No streaming.** The initial `llm:call` surface is request/response only. Streaming will land as a separate subscriber hook so the transport concern can be swapped without touching this plugin's public surface.
- **Error classification is coarse.** Non-transient SDK errors surface as `PluginError({ code: 'unknown' })` — callers can't distinguish 401 from 400 from network loss. Acceptable given the chat loop's current behaviour (surface-and-abort); refine once we have a subscriber that wants to branch on it.

## Boundary review

- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/llm-openai`, `@ax/llm-bedrock`, `@ax/llm-vertex`, `@ax/llm-local-ollama`. Each satisfies the same `llm:call` service hook contract with a different upstream SDK. The hook payload (`LlmRequest` / `LlmResponse`) is deliberately provider-agnostic — no `anthropic_version`, `model_id` shape assumptions, or provider-specific error codes leak upward.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. `messages`, `tools`, `assistantMessage`, `toolCalls` are all LLM-vendor-neutral vocabulary. This plugin converts to/from the Anthropic-specific `content` blocks internally (`type: 'text'` / `type: 'tool_use'`) and never exposes that shape on the hook surface.
- **Subscriber risk:** `llm:call` is a service hook (one producer). No cross-plugin subscribers key off its payload shape today. Future `llm:post-call` subscribers are the designed scrubbing / rewrite lever and will see only the neutral shape.
- **Wire surface:** NOT exposed as an IPC action. `llm:call` is in-process only — the plugin holds the API key, and we do not want that key crossing a sandbox boundary. Any future sandboxed LLM caller would be a separate plugin (e.g., a broker process) talking to the outside world, with this plugin left trusted in the kernel.

content.text:
All 9 coderabbit comments addressed across 4 commits, pushed to PR #5:

**Major fixes (3):**
- `13e687b` — `fix(llm-anthropic)`: system messages now go to Anthropic's top-level `system:` parameter, not `role:'user'`. Previously any `role:'system'` injected via `llm:pre-call` was silently downgraded to a user turn. New test asserts the routing.
- `c5ead01` — `fix(sandbox-subprocess)`: resolve on `'close'` (after stdio drains) instead of `'exit'` (which fires with data possibly still buffered). Prevents trailing-output truncation under load.
- `c312c2c` — `fix(tool-dispatcher)`: coerce undefined `name` in the error message. `JSON.stringify(undefined) === undefined`, so `.slice()` crashed before `PluginError` was constructed — an adversarial payload like `{}` would crash the dispatcher instead of getting a clean `invalid-payload`. Regression test added.

**Minor (6) bundled in `fb15e02`:**
- `any` → `Record<string, unknown>` in framing.test.ts circular fixture
- `eslint-disable-next-line no-restricted-imports` on the cross-plugin sandbox import in bash.test.ts, with rationale pinned at the import site
- dropped unused `root` param from `makeBus` in file-io.test.ts (8 call sites)
- file-level `eslint-disable no-console` in smoke.ts
- MD040 language tags on 4 fenced blocks in SECURITY.md (`console` / `json` / `text`)
- dropped vacuous `not.toMatch(/sk-|key-value/i)` assertion on the init-failed path — the message is a static literal when the key is absent, so the check was moot; key-redaction on failure is still exercised by the 401 test where a real key is present.

Tests: 156 passing across 11 packages (up from 154 — added 1 for system-role routing, 1 for the dispatcher undefined-name crash).
